title: कृष्णाष्टकम्
devanagariText:
वसुदेवसुतं देवं कंसचाणूरमर्दनम्।
देवकीपरमानन्दं कृष्णं वन्दे जगद्गुरुम्॥१॥

अतसीपुष्पसङ्काशं हारनूपुरशोभितम्।
रत्नकङ्कणकेयूरं कृष्णं वन्दे जगद्गुरुम्॥२॥

कुटिलालकसंयुक्तं पूर्णचन्द्रनिभाननम्।
विलसत्कुण्डलधरं कृष्णं वन्दे जगद्गुरुम्॥३॥

मन्दारगन्धसंयुक्तं चारुहासं चतुर्भुजम्।
बर्हिपिञ्छावचूडाङ्गं कृष्णं वन्दे जगद्गुरुम्॥४॥

उत्फुल्लपद्मपत्राक्षं नीलजीमूतसन्निभम्।
यादवानां शिरोरत्नं कृष्णं वन्दे जगद्गुरुम्॥५॥

रुक्मिणीकेलिसंयुक्तं पीताम्बरसुशोभितम्।
अवाप्ततुलसीगन्धं कृष्णं वन्दे जगद्गुरुम्॥६॥

गोपिकानां कुचद्वन्द्वकुङ्कुमाङ्कितवक्षसम्।
श्रीनिकेतं महेष्वासं कृष्णं वन्दे जगद्गुरुम्॥७॥

श्रीवत्साङ्कं महोरस्कं वनमालाविराजितम्।
शङ्खचक्रधरं देवं कृष्णं वन्दे जगद्गुरुम्॥८॥

कृष्णाष्टकमिदं पुण्यं प्रातरुत्थाय यः पठेत्।
कोटिजन्मकृतं पापं स्मरणेन विनश्यति॥
transliteration:
Vasudevasutam Devam Kamsa-Chanura-Mardanam.
Devaki-Paramanandam Krishnam Vande Jagadgurum.||1||

Atasi-Pushpa-Sankasham Hara-Nupura-Shobhitam.
Ratna-Kankana-Keyuram Krishnam Vande Jagadgurum.||2||

Kutila-Alaka-Samyuktam Purna-Chandra-Nibhananam.
Vilasat-Kundala-Dharam Krishnam Vande Jagadgurum.||3||

Mandara-Gandha-Samyuktam Charu-Hasam Chaturbhujam.
Barhi-Pinchava-Chudangam Krishnam Vande Jagadgurum.||4||

Utphulla-Padma-Patraksham Nila-Jimuta-Sannibham.
Yadavanam Shiro-Ratnam Krishnam Vande Jagadgurum.||5||

Rukmini-Keli-Samyuktam Pitambara-Sushobhitam.
Avapta-Tulasi-Gandham Krishnam Vande Jagadgurum.||6||

Gopikanam Kucha-Dvandva-Kunkuma-Ankita-Vakshasam.
Shri-Niketam Maheshvasam Krishnam Vande Jagadgurum.||7||

Shrivatsa-Ankam Mahoraskam Vanamala-Virajitam.
Shankha-Chakra-Dharam Devam Krishnam Vande Jagadgurum.||8||

Krishna-Ashtakam-Idam Punyam Pratar-Utthaya Yah Pathet.
Koti-Janma-Kritam Papam Smaranena Vinashyati.
hindiMeaning:
वसुदेव के पुत्र, देवों के देव, कंस और चाणूर का मर्दन करने वाले, देवकी के परम आनंद स्वरूप, जगद्गुरु श्रीकृष्ण को मैं वंदन करता हूँ।

अलसी के फूल के समान कान्ति वाले, हार और नूपुरों से सुशोभित, रत्न कंगन और केयूर धारण करने वाले जगद्गुरु श्रीकृष्ण को मैं वंदन करता हूँ।

घुंघराले बालों से युक्त, पूर्ण चंद्रमा के समान मुख वाले, विलसित कुंडल धारण करने वाले जगद्गुरु श्रीकृष्ण को मैं वंदन करता हूँ।

मंदार पुष्प की सुगंध से युक्त, सुंदर मुस्कान वाले, चतुर्भुज, मोर पंख का मुकुट धारण करने वाले जगद्गुरु श्रीकृष्ण को मैं वंदन करता हूँ।
benefits: Traditionally recited for removal of sins accumulated over crores of lifetimes
Invokes the blessings and vision of lord krishna
Traditionally associated with peace and purity of mind
Ideal for morning recitation for spiritual upliftment
Traditionally recited for removal of karmic obstacles from the devotee's path
description: Krishna Ashtakam (कृष्णाष्टकम्) is an eight-verse devotional hymn dedicated to Lord Krishna. Rooted in Traditional Sanskrit Stotra, this composition carries deep spiritual significance. Chanting this stotra with devotion is said to bring peace and purity of mind as well as destroy sins accumulated over crores of lifetimes. Additionally, it is known to remove karmic obstacles from the devotee's path. This prayer is traditionally recited on Wednesday (Budhvar) and during Janmashtami to invoke divine grace. With 8 verses and a reading time of about 5 minutes, this prayer is ideal for both daily worship and special occasions. On this page, you can read the complete Krishna Ashtakam in Devanagari Sanskrit with English transliteration, Hindi meaning (arth), and free PDF download for offline recitation.
seoDescription: Read Krishna Ashtakam in Sanskrit with Hindi meaning and transliteration. Eight sacred verses praising Lord Krishna, the Jagadguru.

seoFaqs.question: Who composed the Krishna Ashtakam and how is it structured?
seoFaqs.answer: The Krishna Ashtakam in widest circulation is traditionally attributed to Adi Shankaracharya, the eighth-century founder of the Advaita Vedanta sampradaya. The text is built as eight Sanskrit verses, each closing with the refrain Krishnam Vande Jagadgurum, I bow to Krishna the Guru of the universe. This refrain anchors the whole hymn and gives it its characteristic devotional rhythm. A separate, longer Krishnashtakam beginning Bhaje Vrajaikamandanam is sometimes also called Krishna Ashtakam in Vaishnava sources, but the Shankara version with the Jagadguru refrain is the one most commonly chanted in Smarta and Advaita households.

seoFaqs.question: What does the refrain Krishnam Vande Jagadgurum signify?
seoFaqs.answer: Jagadguru means teacher of the universe. By closing each verse with Krishnam Vande Jagadgurum, Shankara identifies Krishna not merely as the lovable cowherd of Vrindavan but as the universal teacher whose Bhagavad Gita instruction to Arjuna is the source-stream of dharma for all later seekers. The refrain functions like a mantra threaded through the hymn, ensuring that even when the verses dwell on tender lila scenes such as Yashoda nursing the child, the listener never forgets Krishna's identity as the supreme guru. This pairing of intimate bhakti with cosmic jnana is a signature of Shankara's stotras.

seoFaqs.question: What scenes from Krishna's life does the ashtakam praise?
seoFaqs.answer: The eight verses move through the well-loved chapters of Krishna's biography: his birth as the divine son of Vasudeva and Devaki in Kamsa's prison, his transfer across the Yamuna to Yashoda and Nanda in Gokul, his childhood pranks with butter and the gopis, his slaying of demons including Putana, Trinavarta, Aghasura, Kamsa and Chanura, his lifting of Govardhana, and his role as Arjuna's charioteer and guru in the Mahabharata. The closing verses fold these episodes back into Krishna's identity as the unchanging Brahman, the same reality that wears the peacock feather and plays the flute under kadamba trees.

seoFaqs.question: How does Krishna Ashtakam differ from Govinda Ashtakam and Achyuta Ashtakam?
seoFaqs.answer: All three are eight-verse hymns to Krishna in the Adi Shankara stotra corpus, and many devotees chant them together. Govinda Ashtakam emphasises the cowherd, Vrindavan and Govardhana imagery and closes with a phala-shruti about crossing samsara. Krishna Ashtakam emphasises the Jagadguru identity, with the refrain placing the focus on Krishna as universal teacher. Achyuta Ashtakam (Achyuta meaning the unfallen) emphasises Krishna's unwavering, infallible nature and is often chanted as a quick daily prayer. Together they form a tight devotional cluster that traverses Krishna's pastoral, royal and metaphysical aspects in three short hymns.

seoFaqs.question: When is Krishna Ashtakam traditionally chanted?
seoFaqs.answer: Janmashtami, the eighth day of the dark fortnight of Bhadrapada celebrating Krishna's midnight birth, is the principal occasion. Devotees often chant the ashtakam at the moment of midnight aarti together with the Bhagavad Gita's tenth chapter (Vibhuti Yoga) or selected verses of the Bhagavata Purana's tenth canto. Beyond Janmashtami, the ashtakam is chanted on Ekadashi days dedicated to Vishnu, on every Wednesday (associated with Vaishnava worship in some traditions), during the months of Kartika and Margashirsha, and as a daily morning prayer in many Smarta households. Its short length makes it ideal for combining with longer practices.

seoFaqs.question: What benefits do practitioners traditionally associate with Krishna Ashtakam?
seoFaqs.answer: Traditional sources link sincere recitation with the cultivation of bhakti (devotion), the dissolving of papas (karmic burdens) through divine remembrance, and steady progress toward the ultimate fruit of moksha as taught in the Gita. Devotees also report psychological benefits such as a calmer mind in the morning, easier release of resentments through the act of bowing to Jagadguru, and a clearer sense of dharma in daily decisions. Because the ashtakam closes each verse with bowing rather than asking, it cultivates the bhava of surrender (sharanagati) that Vaishnava traditions hold as the surest doorway to grace.
